name: Kurma Purana
shortDescription: A Hindu Purana dedicated to Vishnu's Kurma avatar, detailing creation, dharma, and cosmology.
description: The Kurma Purana is one of the eighteen major Puranas of Hinduism. Classified as a Tamas Purana (related to darkness, inertia), it primarily focuses on Vishnu's Kurma (tortoise) avatar. The text is structured as a discourse delivered by Vishnu in his Kurma form to King Indradyumna. It covers a wide range of topics, including cosmology, genealogy of kings and sages, descriptions of holy places, the duties of the four varnas (social classes), and the importance of dharma. It also contains narratives about Vishnu's various incarnations and the battles between gods and demons. While centered on Vishnu, the Kurma Purana also includes sections dedicated to Shiva and Shakti, reflecting the syncretic nature of Hinduism. The text exists in various versions, with significant variations in content and the number of chapters.
subtitle: The Tortoise Incarnation: Wisdom from the Depths
author: Traditionally attributed to Vyasa
significance: Provides insights into Vaishnava theology, Hindu cosmology, and social ethics.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Kurma Purana derives its name from Vishnu's second avatar, Kurma, the tortoise. The most prominent myth associated with the Purana is the Samudra Manthana, the churning of the ocean. In this story, the gods and demons unite to churn the ocean of milk to obtain amrita (the nectar of immortality). Mount Mandara serves as the churning rod, and Vasuki, the serpent, acts as the rope. As the mountain begins to sink, Vishnu incarnates as Kurma, providing a stable base upon which the mountain rests. This allows the churning process to continue, ultimately leading to the emergence of amrita, Lakshmi (the goddess of fortune), and other celestial treasures. The Purana also recounts stories of other Vishnu avatars, such as Rama and Krishna, albeit with less emphasis than in other Vaishnava Puranas.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Kurma Purana plays a significant role in Vaishnava Hinduism, providing theological insights into the nature of Vishnu and his avatars. It outlines the duties and responsibilities of individuals within the framework of dharma, emphasizing the importance of righteous conduct, devotion, and adherence to Vedic principles. The text's descriptions of sacred places (tirthas) contribute to the understanding of Hindu pilgrimage traditions. By detailing the genealogies of kings and sages, the Kurma Purana helps to establish a historical and cultural context for Hindu beliefs and practices. The inclusion of narratives about Shiva and Shakti underscores the syncretic nature of Hinduism, highlighting the interconnectedness of different deities and traditions.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Kurma avatar symbolizes stability, support, and perseverance. The tortoise's ability to withdraw into its shell represents the importance of introspection and self-control. The churning of the ocean symbolizes the process of spiritual transformation, where both positive and negative forces are necessary for achieving enlightenment. Amrita represents immortality and spiritual liberation, while the various treasures that emerge from the ocean symbolize the diverse aspects of the divine. The Kurma Purana, therefore, can be interpreted as a guide to achieving spiritual growth through devotion, righteous conduct, and self-awareness. The text's emphasis on dharma highlights the importance of ethical behavior and social responsibility in the pursuit of spiritual liberation.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Kurma Purana continues to be studied and revered by Hindus today. Its narratives and teachings have influenced various aspects of Hindu art, literature, and philosophy. The story of the Samudra Manthana is a popular theme in Hindu iconography, often depicted in sculptures and paintings. The text's emphasis on dharma and righteous conduct continues to guide the ethical behavior of many Hindus. Modern translations and interpretations of the Kurma Purana have made its teachings accessible to a wider audience, contributing to its continued relevance in contemporary Hindu society. The text's syncretic approach, incorporating elements of both Vaishnavism and Shaivism, reflects the inclusive and adaptable nature of Hinduism.

sources.author: G.V. Tagare
sources.description: English translation and annotation of the Kurma Purana published by All India Kashiraj Trust.

sources.author: Mani, Vettam
sources.description: A comprehensive dictionary of characters and concepts from the Puranas.

associations.name: Tortoise
associations.significance: Represents Vishnu's Kurma avatar, stability and support.

associations.name: Ocean
associations.significance: The location of the Samudra Manthana, representing the source of creation.

associations.name: Dharma
associations.significance: A central theme in the Purana, emphasizing righteous conduct and social responsibility.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Atlas
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both Kurma and Atlas support a great weight (the churning mountain/the heavens).

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Ao
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Ao is a turtle who supports the world.

companions.name: Vishnu
companions.description: Kurma is the second avatar of Vishnu, the preserver god.

companions.name: Vasuki
companions.description: The serpent king used as the churning rope in the Samudra Manthana.

companions.name: Lakshmi
companions.description: Goddess of fortune who emerged during Samudra Manthana.

worshipPractices.name: Vishnu Puja
worshipPractices.description: Worship of Vishnu, often including recitation of Puranic stories like those found in the Kurma Purana.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Visits
worshipPractices.description: Visiting temples dedicated to Vishnu or his avatars, where the Kurma avatar may be depicted.

festivals.name: Kurma Jayanti
festivals.description: Celebrates the birth of Vishnu's Kurma avatar.

festivals.name: Samudra Manthan Celebrations
festivals.description: Enactments and celebrations of the churning of the ocean.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Kurma Purana continues to inspire art, literature, and philosophical discourse, informing Hindu identity and cultural practices globally.
